description: This is a research presentation (SS27: MIS - Cool Stuff) from SAGES 2017 by Arun Nemani, PhD on non-invasive brain imaging of laparoscopic motor skills learning using fNIRS (functional near-infrared spectroscopy). The video consists entirely of presentation slides with no FLS task performance or demonstration.

frame_analyses.content: Title slide - SS27: MIS - Cool Stuff, Introduction slide on brain imaging for laparoscopic motor skills learning
Introduction - FLS: Overview of FLS as validated testing platform for board certification
Introduction - Challenges in current metrics: discussion of time/error-based criteria limitations
Introduction - Brain imaging methods: Near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) technical details
Introduction - Motor skill learning: neurophysiological pathways, PFC, M1, SMA regions
Methods (Experimental Setup): subjects (9 surgical novices, 8 surgical experts), FLS pattern cutting task, 5 trials
Methods (Hardware and data processing): Techen CW6 continuous wave infrared spectroscopy system, HOMER2 software, LDA analysis
FLS task performance: Mann-Whitney U test results showing significant differentiation between novice and expert (p<0.05), cross-validation classification results
FLS pattern cutting task performance: detailed brain activation analysis by cortical region (PFC, M1, SMA) comparing novice vs expert
Classification results: PDFs for fNIRS metrics, LDA projected scores showing expert/novice separation, MCE metrics
Spatial maps of cortical activation: comparative brain maps showing activation patterns in surgical novices vs surgical experts
Conclusions: three research questions answered affirmatively regarding fNIRS ability to differentiate and classify surgical motor skills

scoring_note: No FLS task performance visible. This is an educational/research presentation, not a trainee or expert demonstration of an actual FLS task. Per instructions, instructional content with narration (implicit in slides) and research data should not be scored as an FLS performance.
reason: Video contains no FLS task execution. Content is a research presentation on a study investigating brain imaging correlates of surgical motor skill using FLS pattern cutting as the baseline task, but no actual task performance is recorded or demonstrated in the slides.

teaching_content.research_focus: Use of functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) as a non-invasive biomarker for objectively assessing surgical motor skill acquisition
key_findings: fNIRS can objectively differentiate surgical motor skills between expert and novice surgeons in real time (p<0.05)
Classification of expert vs. novice achievable with <5% misclassification error using cross-validated LDA
Cortical activation patterns differ significantly in prefrontal cortex (PFC), left medial M1, and supplementary motor area (SMA) between skill levels
Study demonstrates potential for objective, metrics-based surgical skill assessment beyond traditional time and error-based criteria

final_note: This video is clearly research presentation material and does not contain FLS task performance data suitable for scoring under the FLS rubric. No performance score or penalty assessment is applicable.
